name: Aereo
summary: Aereo let subscribers stream local broadcast TV via tiny remote antennas, bypassing retransmission fees. The Supreme Court ruled this violated copyright law, forcing immediate shutdown.
lesson: Building a media business on an untested legal loophole is a bet on courts, not customers.

name: MoviePass
summary: A subscription service offering nearly unlimited movie theater tickets for a flat monthly fee; the company paid full price for every ticket redeemed, resulting in catastrophic losses.
lesson: A subscription price below your per-unit cost of goods sold is not a growth strategy, it's a countdown to insolvency.

name: Quibi
summary: Short-form premium streaming service raised $1.75B for mobile-only shows but shut down just six months after launch amid the pandemic and weak demand.
lesson: Massive funding and star power cannot substitute for validating that people actually want the format.

name: CNN+
summary: WarnerMedia's standalone CNN streaming news service launched with major star hires, then was shut down after just three weeks amid the Warner Bros. Discovery merger and low subscriber numbers.
lesson: Launching a major product days before a change of corporate ownership invites it being killed regardless of its own merits.

name: Redbox
summary: The DVD-kiosk giant tried to pivot into streaming as physical rental declined, was acquired by Chicken Soup for the Soul Entertainment in 2022, and filed for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in 2024.
lesson: A legacy hardware business rarely out-runs the decline of its core product just by bolting on a streaming app.

name: Weinstein Company
summary: A major independent film and TV studio co-founded by Harvey and Bob Weinstein; it collapsed into bankruptcy within months of the New York Times investigation exposing Harvey Weinstein's sexual assault and harassment.
lesson: Founder misconduct can destroy an entire company's value virtually overnight, regardless of its content library or track record.

name: Relativity Media
summary: Ryan Kavanaugh's film and TV studio used complex financing structures to fund a rapid slate of movies, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2015, and collapsed again in 2018 under unsustainable debt.
lesson: Financial engineering can fund rapid growth but doesn't fix an underlying business that loses money on its core product.

name: iflix
summary: A Netflix-style streaming service targeting emerging markets raised over $300M but was sold off for parts to Tencent in 2020 after failing to reach profitability.
lesson: Emerging-market streaming requires enormous capital to build content and infrastructure against well-funded global rivals.

name: STX Entertainment
summary: A film and TV studio that raised hundreds of millions, went public via SPAC in 2020, saw its stock collapse, and was ultimately absorbed into Lionsgate in a distressed 2023 deal.
lesson: A SPAC listing can provide capital and a public valuation but doesn't fix a studio's underlying content economics.

name: Vine
summary: Twitter's six-second looping video app was a cultural phenomenon, but Twitter failed to build a creator monetization program and lost ground to Instagram and Snapchat.
lesson: A viral content format without a creator payout mechanism will lose its best creators to platforms that pay.

name: Machinima
summary: A gaming-focused multi-channel network, majority-owned by Warner Bros., was shut down abruptly in 2019 with mass layoffs after failing to build a sustainable advertising revenue model.
lesson: MCN aggregation of creator content struggled to build durable margins once platforms captured ad revenue directly.

name: Rooster Teeth
summary: A pioneering internet content company with a fiercely loyal fan community was shut down in 2024 by Warner Bros. Discovery after 21 years amid broader corporate cost-cutting.
lesson: A profitable niche community-driven brand can still be cut when a parent conglomerate prioritizes company-wide cost reduction.

name: National CineMedia
summary: The largest cinema advertising network in the US filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2023 due to a heavy debt burden and slower-than-expected post-COVID theater attendance recovery.
lesson: Even a market-leading position can't offset a debt structure built for pre-pandemic attendance levels.

name: Participant Media
summary: A social-impact film and TV studio (Spotlight, An Inconvenient Truth) ceased operations in 2023 after nearly two decades and dozens of Oscar-nominated films.
lesson: A prestigious, mission-driven content strategy can still be financially unsustainable as distribution economics shift under streaming.

name: Netflix
summary: Started as a DVD-by-mail rental service, Netflix pivoted decisively to streaming in 2007 and later to original content, becoming the dominant global subscription video platform.
lesson: Willingness to cannibalize your own profitable core business for an emerging technology can define an entire industry.

name: Hulu
summary: Launched jointly by NBCUniversal and News Corp as a legal streaming alternative to piracy, Hulu built a hybrid ad-supported and premium subscription model.
lesson: A joint venture between rival media companies can succeed when the shared threat (piracy) outweighs competitive instincts.

name: Disney+
summary: Disney's direct-to-consumer streaming service launched with deep back catalogs from Marvel, Star Wars, Pixar, and Disney Animation, reaching 150M+ subscribers.
lesson: Decades of beloved intellectual property, pulled back from licensing deals, can launch a streaming service to scale almost instantly.

name: Roku
summary: Originally incubated as a Netflix streaming-device project, Roku spun out independently and became the leading streaming device and smart TV operating system platform.
lesson: Owning the hardware/software layer that other streaming services depend on can be more durable than competing with them directly.

name: Tubi
summary: A free ad-supported streaming (FAST) service that built a large content library through licensing rather than expensive originals, acquired by Fox Corporation in 2020 for $440M.
lesson: A free, ad-supported model with licensed content can scale profitably in a market oversaturated with paid subscriptions.

name: Twitch
summary: A pivot from the failing general live-streaming site Justin.tv, Twitch focused specifically on video game live streaming and became the dominant platform, acquired by Amazon in 2014.
lesson: Narrowing a failing general-purpose platform down to the single vertical that was actually working can unlock enormous value.

name: Crunchyroll
summary: Started as a fan-driven anime streaming site, Crunchyroll became the dominant global anime platform and was consolidated with Funimation under Sony.
lesson: Deep focus on an underserved but passionate fan community can build a defensible market position major studios eventually pay a premium to own.

name: A24
summary: An independent studio built a reputation for distinctive, critically acclaimed films and expanded into television and a strong consumer merchandise brand.
lesson: A sharply defined creative identity can become a durable brand and business moat in a commoditized film industry.
